questions.question: Compare Ethereum and Bitcoin in terms of their purpose, scripting capability, transaction model, and consensus mechanism.
keyPoints: Purpose: Bitcoin is digital currency/store of value; Ethereum is a programmable blockchain platform for smart contracts and dApps.
Scripting: Bitcoin Script is limited and not Turing-complete; Ethereum supports Turing-complete languages (Solidity, Vyper).
Transaction model: Bitcoin uses UTXO model; Ethereum uses account-based model with balances and nonces.
Consensus: Bitcoin uses Proof of Work (SHA-256); Ethereum transitioned to Proof of Stake after The Merge (2022).

questions.question: Explain the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM), its key properties, and its role in the Ethereum ecosystem.
keyPoints: Definition: The EVM is a runtime environment for executing smart contract bytecode on every Ethereum node.
Deterministic: Same input always produces the same output across all nodes.
Sandboxed: Contracts run in isolation; they cannot access network, filesystem, or other external resources.
Stack-based: Uses a last-in, first-out (LIFO) stack architecture for computation.
Gas-metered: Every operation costs gas, preventing infinite loops and denial-of-service attacks.
Role: Ensures all nodes reach the same state by executing the same bytecode with the same results.
Pipeline: Solidity source code is compiled to bytecode, which the EVM then executes.

questions.question: Differentiate between Externally Owned Accounts (EOA) and Contract Accounts in Ethereum.
keyPoints: EOA: Controlled by private keys held by users; can initiate transactions; has no code; has address, balance, and nonce.
Contract Account: Controlled by smart contract code; cannot initiate transactions independently (must be triggered); has code and persistent storage.
Only EOAs can start a transaction chain; contract accounts respond to being called.
Both have addresses, balances, and nonces, but only contracts have codeHash and storageRoot.

questions.question: What is gas in Ethereum? Explain its purpose, how gas fees are calculated, and what happens when a transaction runs out of gas.
keyPoints: Gas is a unit measuring computational work; every EVM operation has a defined gas cost.
Purpose: prevents infinite loops, pays validators, measures computation costs.
Fee calculation: Transaction Fee = Gas Used x Gas Price (legacy) or Gas Used x (Base Fee + Priority Fee) under EIP-1559.
Out of gas: transaction fails, all state changes are reverted, but gas consumed up to that point is NOT refunded.
